learning_outcomes.description: Identify the importance of stakeholders in issue and crisis management.
Identify the importance of understanding and using a range of media and communication tactics to plan for and achieve different outcomes.
Demonstrate an understanding of the concepts, theories and practice of issues management and crisis communication.
Apply the theories, methods and models of issues and crisis management to identify, track and respond to real life and simulated scenarios.
Identify and apply ethical standards of practice when dealing with issues management and crisis communication.
Ability to work collaboratively with other members of a team to apply the theories, methods, and models of issues and crisis management in real-life and simulated scenarios.

assessments.task_description:
Active Engagement Quizzes

Designed to encourage continuous engagement with course content and track business developments throughout the semester, this assessment uses a series of short in-lecture quizzes as regular learning checkpoints. Students complete 10 quizzes across the semester, with only their best 5 results contributing to the final grade (5 quizzes × 4% each = 20%). This "best performance" model provides inherent flexibility while encouraging students to remain actively engaged with weekly learning activities and ongoing developments in the world relevant to issues and crisis communication practice.

Drawing on a pedagogy of active and applied learning, the quizzes require students to engage with concepts, theories, readings, case studies, and contemporary examples discussed in lectures and tutorials. Questions draw upon course readings and ongoing developments in business, politics, society, and the media, and are designed to encourage students to apply disciplinary concepts to authentic situations rather than simply recalling information. This is particularly important in communication and media industry, where the ability to recognise, interpret, and respond to emerging developments in real time is a core professional skill.

Moreover, post-quiz discussion and reflection will make it a feed-back rich assessment. Quiz results are used to identify areas of misunderstanding, stimulate discussion, and revisit challenging concepts. The assessment functions not only as a mechanism for measuring learning but also as a catalyst for further learning. By combining short, low-stakes assessment with immediate discussion and clarification, and further elaboration in tutorials, students receive regular opportunities to test their understanding, refine their thinking, and strengthen their ability to apply course concepts to real-world issues and crisis scenarios.

The "best 5 of 10" structure is intentionally designed to accommodate the realities of student life, including occasional illness, competing commitments, and other short-term disruptions. Quizzes are completed during scheduled in-person lecture sessions to preserve a common assessment experience and the integrity of the task.

Academic Integrity

Students complete quizzes individually during scheduled in-person lecture sessions. Questions are contextualised to specific course materials, discussions, case studies, and scenarios encountered during the semester, requiring students to apply their understanding rather than reproduce memorised information. Questions are drawn from a randomised pool and answer options are shuffled, ensuring no two students receive an identical quiz experience. The accompanying post-quiz discussions provide further opportunities for staff to observe student understanding and engagement. The ongoing nature of the assessment generates multiple demonstrations of learning across the semester and reduces reliance on any single assessment event.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission information will be posted on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Underpinned by a pedagogy of active and applied learning, this assessment requires students to critically evaluate how organisations communicate during issues and crises by applying concepts, theories and models introduced throughout the course. The assessment is designed to bridge academic learning and professional practice by requiring students to analyse real-world communication materials and develop evidence-based recommendations for improvement.

Students select a real organisational communication response to an issue or crisis, such as a press release, CEO statement, social media post, video message, media interview, or another public communication. They critically evaluate whether the communication response was successful in achieving its objectives using relevant theories, empirical research, and available evidence including media coverage, audience reactions and organisational outcomes.

In addition to describing or critiquing the response, students demonstrate how the communication strategy could be strengthened. Depending on the case, students may present a revised press release, social media post, video response, CEO statement, media strategy, or another communication response that better addresses the issue or crisis. Students should justify how their revised response is informed by communication theory, empirical evidence and professional practice.

Each student delivers a five-minute presentation followed by a short question-and-answer session with the tutor and classmates. The discussion provides students with an opportunity to explain and defend their recommendations while demonstrating their understanding of course concepts and their application to real-world communication challenges.

This assessment develops students' ability to apply theories and models of issues and crisis management to analyse contemporary communication challenges, evaluate organisational responses, and communicate evidence-based recommendations in a professional context.

Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment

All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device, audio recorder)

Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.

Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of:

  • Moderation of marking;
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.


Students must acknowledge all use of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine translation (MT) and remain updated with the advice and best practices as prescribed by the university.

assessments.due_date:
Week 9 Tue - Week 10 Fri

Presentation: In person during Week 9 and 10 tutorials.
assessments.task_description:
The goal of the assignment is to help students identify and critically analyse an organisational crisis. Based on theories and best practices learned in the class, students then provide recommendations on how to better respond to a similar crisis in future.

Working in groups, students will present a crisis case study, followed by a question-and-answer session to test their understanding and depth of knowledge related to the subject. Students will also be tested to 'think on their feet' to address alternative crisis scenarios. Points will be provided for both content and delivery, and individual students' contributions to the group work.

The presentation will provide an overview of the crisis and critically examine the organisation's responses and their impact on media coverage and stakeholder responses. Finally, it will offer key recommendations on communicating more effectively in future crises.

Students should use a mix of scholarly references, media coverage, and organisational materials. The student presentations will occur in tutorials. Following the presentation, each student will submit a reflection on their learning outcomes and contribution to group work.

The task sheet/rubric for the essay is available on Blackboard.

Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment 

All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)  

Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.  

Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: 

  • Moderation of marking;  
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or  
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Presentation: In person during Week 9 and 10 tutorials.
